metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_746
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Conclusion: "Our study supports the growing consensus that Cplxs (Cplx3 in RBs and Cplx1 and Cplx2 at conventional synapses) act to stabilize fully primed vesicles in a release-ready state and to suppress unregulated Ca2+-dependent spontaneous and asynchronous release of immature vesicles at early stages of the priming process."

Electrophysiological recordings in mouse retina tissue analyzing wildtype- and Complexin-3 KO mice. Mouse retinal bipolar cells only express Complexin-3 and are therefore a good synapse to study the function of this protein (it is known that Complexins are partially redundant and can compensate for the loss of one isoform).

The study demonstrates a loss of phasic release (reduced EPSC amplitude; Fig.1), increase in spontaneous release (mEPSC frequency Fig.2+6) and asynchronous release (Fig. 7). Impaired phasic release was manifested both in a reduced functional RRP and in an increased EPSC latency. The diminished functional RRP likely resulted from the absence of a facilitatory effect of Cplx3 on Ca2+-dependentexocytosis (Fig. 4) rather than from physical vesicle depletion as assessed by electron microscopy (Fig. S1).. Experimental description: Retinas from dark-adapted Cplx3-/-, littermate Cplx3+/+, or C57B6 mice (1-6 months old) (Reim et al., 2009) were isolated...
